experience: Always filter dominated points, rank with a cheap vectorized proxy, and keep an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs and use CELF/timestamps to avoid repeat HV calls. Limit HV budget and swap iterations, reuse buffers (avoid vstack per-candidate), and only precompute pairwise HVs when shortlist is tiny. Prioritize deterministic tie-breaks and a small, well‑tuned shortlist for consistent high-quality tradeoffs.
current_reflection: Always filter dominated points, rank with a cheap vectorized proxy, and keep an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs and use CELF/timestamps to avoid repeat HV calls. Limit HV budget and swap iterations, reuse buffers (avoid vstack per-candidate), and only precompute pairwise HVs when shortlist is tiny. Prioritize deterministic tie-breaks and a small, well‑tuned shortlist for consistent high-quality tradeoffs.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Design deterministically. Pareto‑prefilter, score with box+adaptive scalar probes, make shortlist O(k) (scale with pareto size), use CELF lazy greedy with singleton HV cache and an explicit HV‑call budget, prune with box-volume upper bounds, avoid pairwise caches when shortlist>~40, use sets/masks for membership, minimize vstacking by reusing buffers, and do a tiny, targeted swap pass on a small candidate pool.

Design heuristics that (1) vectorize all cheap proxies and nondominated filtering, (2) adapt shortlist size to k and true Pareto size, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with singleton cache + tight box upper bounds, (4) restrict pairwise caching to tiny shortlists, and (5) limit swap candidates/iterations. Measure and cap exact-HV calls; prefer deterministic sets/arrays to avoid Python-level membership and repeated vstack overhead.

Design heuristics that minimize exact HV calls: vectorize prefiltering/scoring, build an adaptive O(k) shortlist, use CELF with singleton caches and timestamps, apply box‑volume upper bounds and only small selective pairwise caching, then run a tightly bounded deterministic swap. Tune shortlist size and weight-vector count to trade quality vs HV budget; preallocate buffers and prefer set membership + vector ops to cut Python loop overhead.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: dominated filtering, vectorized proxies, O(k) shortlist, singleton HV cache, CELF timestamps, box-volume bounds, pairwise cache, HV budget, deterministic ties
- Advice: Prefilter nondominated points, rank with a few vectorized weight probes, keep an O(k) shortlist, apply CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and singleton HV cache, prune with box bounds, restrict pairwise caching to tiny shortlists, cap exact-HV calls and swap iterations, reuse buffers and enforce deterministic tie-breaks
- Avoid: repeated per-candidate HV recomputations, Python-level vstack per candidate, nondeterministic ordering, unlimited pairwise caches or swap loops
- Explanation: These choices minimize costly HV evaluations and Python overhead, yielding fast, consistent, high-quality k-subsets
